coredata.dc:title: Autonomous magnetic microbots for environmental remediation developed by organic waste derived carbon dots

Gautam, Pavan Kumar
Verma, Arushi
Maurya, Krishna
Sk, Md Palashuddin
Samanta, Sintu Kumar
Sahoo, Amaresh Kumar
coredata.dcterms:subject: Micromotors
Carbon dots
Water treatment
Dye removal
Nanozyme
Environmental remediation

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors are thankful for the financial support from DBT, Govt. of India (SANCTION No. BT/PR40544/COD/139/14/2020). Author, also acknowledges the Department of Chemistry [DRS-II(SAP), DST (FIST & PURSE) funded] and University Sophisticated Instrument Facility (USIF), Aligarh Muslim University. Dr. Pavan Kumar Gautam greatful to Science and Engineering Research Board, Govt. of India for the NPDF (Grant No. PDF/2016/002910).

Authors are thankful for the financial support from DBT , Govt. of India (SANCTION No. BT/PR40544/COD/139/14/2020 ). Author, also acknowledges the Department of Chemistry [ DRS-II(SAP) , DST (FIST & PURSE) funded] and University Sophisticated Instrument Facility (USIF), Aligarh Muslim University . Dr. Pavan Kumar Gautam greatful to Science and Engineering Research Board , Govt. of India for the NPDF (Grant No. PDF/2016/002910 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the microbot developed from organic waste-based C-Dots that has the potential to move autonomously through liquid medium and can efficiently degrade toxic dyes present in it. This scheme explains the principle of microbots functioning and performance.

a) TEM image of C-Dots, showing spherical particle having size less than 10 nm b) TEM image of microbots showing presence of both Fe3O4 NPs as well as C-dots, inset is HRTEM showing the lattice planes of crystalline iron oxide NPs with the lattice spacing of 0.27 nm whereas C-Dot did not show any such characteristic as these are amorphous that surrounded around Fe3O4 NPs; (c–e) High resolution XPS spectra of C-Dots showing peak positions of C1s, O1s and N1s.

Autonomous movement of the microbots; (a) snapshot of the lateral movement of the microbot at different time intervals in 2% (v/v) H2O2 fuel solution. This video was recorded under stereo zoom microscope with 1 mm grid; (b) trajectory of the microbot movement in 2% (v/v) H2O2 fuel solution; (c) graph showing the change in the speed on the microbot with increasing H2O2 concentration, the data represents mean ± SD of three individual experiments; (d) snapshot of the vertical movement of the microbot at different time interval in 1% (v/v) H2O2 fuel solution; (e) graph showing average vertical movement cycles of the microbot with increasing H2O2 concentration, the data represents mean ± SD of three individual experiments.

(a) Percentage degradation of 10 ppm MG dye using microbots in 1% (v/v) H2O2 fuel solution, the data represents mean ± SD of three individual experiments; (b) percentage degradation of 10 ppm Rh–B dye using microbots in 1% (v/v) H2O2 fuel solution, the data represents mean ± SD of three individual experiments; (c) schematics of change in activation energy when microbots were used for the degradation of MG dye; (d) Percentage degradation of the dye mixture of 10 ppm MG and 10 ppm Rh–B dye using microbots in 1% (v/v) H2O2 fuel solution.

(a) Comparative study of percentage degradation of MG dye under different viscosity in 2 h; the data represents mean ± SD of three individual experiments; (b) reusability of the microbots. The data represents mean ± SD of three individual experiments.

head.ce:title: Autonomous magnetic microbots for environmental remediation developed by organic waste derived carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Sciences, Indian Institute of Information Technology Allahabad, Prayagraj, UP, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Indian Institute of Information Technology Allahabad

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Sciences, Indian Institute of Information Technology Allahabad, Prayagraj, UP, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Aligarh Muslim University, Aligarh, UP, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Aligarh Muslim University, Aligarh, UP, India

ce:section.ce:para: Globally water pollution level is at an all-time high, predominantly due to the unrestrained urbanization and enormous volume of streaming of waste from various industrial sectors including textile effluents. Generally, textile effluents contain a substantial amount of synthetic toxic dyes which offers a huge threat to human health and the environment (Khan and Malik, 2018). Thus, immediate measures and precautions are very essential to avoid unprecedented consequences and safeguard the future. Recently, several chemical, electrical and physical methods have been explored by various researchers to address this severe challenge (Mishra et al., 2019; Kumar et al., 2018b; Reddy et al., 2019). In this regard, uses of microbots– autonomous catalytic ‘manmade’ nano/microscale objects– that ought to be functional in constrained environments offer a great deal of scope. These futuristic devices are imagined to execute ‘programmed’ actions both in biological and chemical environments more precisely and efficiently. Moreover, the unique design and composition of the microbot decide its characteristic of propulsion and catalytic behaviour. Therefore, that might be deployed for toxic dye degradation and/or removal from contaminated water. Several model systems have been demonstrated by various research groups where bots with nanoscale materials harvested the chemical energy for its motion by catalytic decomposition of the aqueous hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) (Su et al., 2019; Shivalkar et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2019; Rayaroth et al., 2021). In this case, the magnitude and direction of the movement of the bots could be monitored easily by employing physical and chemical stimuli (Bhati et al., 2016; Chhabra et al., 2019; aS. Kumar et al., 2018a; Das et al., 2019). Recently, the use of metal nanoparticles (e.g., Pt, Ni, Pd and Au) as catalytic agents showed promising outcomes (Dhakshinamoorthy et al., 2012). However, the cost, complex method of synthesis, stability in the highly oxidizing environment, biocompatibility and reusability of the metal nanoparticles (NPs) raises several issues in dye degradation. Moreover, these metal NPs release metal ions during operation of microbots that eventually demand further extra steps in water purifications which ultimately limit their usages. Therefore, development of a viable alternative of metal NPs based microbots is essential, which will offer the scope of being functional in the harsh environment, biocompatible and economical that are essential prerequisites for large scale water treatment. Recently, different protocols and materials have been explored for the fabrication of micro/nano swimmers for water remediation (bS. Kumar et al., 2018c). In most of the works, the combination of multi-metallic components has been used for locomotion, catalytic purpose and magnet-dependent navigation. However, the techniques used to combine different metals such as electric deposition, laser induced deposition require high energy consumption and complex setup which increases the fabrication cost. These facts intrigued us to develop efficient microbot with economic and environmental feasibility. The design of the present C-Dots functionalized Fe3O4 microbot is very simple as the magnetic component of the bot has been synthesized by coprecipitation. And the produced C-Dots are organic and biodegradable. Additionally, the bots have been designed without using any volatile solvents and synthetic reactants. Therefore, the present bots don't cause any secondary contamination.

Herein, we report the development of microbots using carbon dots (C-Dots) synthesized by using organic household waste and iron oxide nanoparticles (Fe3O4 NPs). A microwave-based fast and simple one-step synthesis of fluorescent C-Dots was developed by using potato peels as a precursor. It would be worthwhile to mention here that fluorescent C-Dots are quasi-spherical nanoparticles that show unique physicochemical properties such as excitation tunable emission, high solubility in an aqueous medium, superior chemical inertness and better photo-stability. It also served as donor and acceptor of electrons relying on the reaction condition, which makes it an effective nano-catalyst (Verma et al., 2020; Shi et al., 2011). Moreover, C-Dots are found to be biocompatible as compared to other nanoscale materials, hence suitable for various biomedical applications. Our results demonstrated that microbots synthesized based on C-Dots not only catalyses the decomposition of H2O2 but also promoted the degradation of hydrophilic (e.g., Malachite green and Rhodamine-B) as well as sparingly soluble dyes (e.g., Sudan-I). As a model system, malachite green (MG) – a commonly used dye in textile and paper industries was opted to determine the rate kinetics and thermodynamic parameters of dye degradation by the microbots. It would be mentioned here that MG dye is toxic in even at very low concentration (i.e., IC50: half maximal inhibitory concentration is 2 μM) for mammalian cells (Gopinathan et al., 2015). Also, recent in-vivo studies found that this dye showed a carcinogenic and mutagenic effect (Akhtar et al., 2016). Therefore, water contamination with MG dye raises huge concern and draws significant attention from various research groups that helped to develop several methods such as membrane filtration, ion exchange, chemical precipitation and adsorption for removal of such toxic dyes (Crini et al., 2006). However, these processes are time-consuming, require complex operational setup, and expensive which causes the unlikelihood of using these for industrial applications. The present microbots were able to catalytically self-propel with an averages speed of 86.11 ± 10 μm/s in an aqueous medium in presence of 2% (v/v) hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and degraded dyes effectively, thus found to be very promising for water purification. Fig. 1 is a schematic representation of the overall strategy of development as well as the performance of the microbots.

C-Dots were prepared from domestic household waste potato (Solanum tuberosum) peel. A substantial amount of Potato Peel was washed with DI water and dried in a Hot Air Oven for 48 h at 50 °C. Later crushed it was down to maximum possible finest particles. 1 g of these crushed particles of potato peel was taken into a beaker with 400 μL of NH4Cl (Ammonium Chloride) and 400 μL of NaOH (Sodium Hydroxide) added into it. The mixture was kept under microwave irradiation and 10 s pulse heating was provided until it became black. 20 mL of DI water was added and kept it for continuous stirring for half an hour and formation of a dark brown coloured solution that was showing strong blue emission under UV light exposure is an indication of formation of C-Dots. Later it was filtered to remove unreacted debris; finally, filtrate was dialyzed for 24 h in DI water to collect pure C-dots.

For the synthesis of iron oxide nanoparticles, ferric chloride and ferrous chloride were taken into 2:1 M ratio (324 mg and 126.8 mg respectively) with 10 mL of DI water and kept for stirring under mild heating. Later, 4 mL of 25% ammonium hydroxide was added into the solution and black tiny particles instantly formed found attracting towards the magnet. This indicates the formation of magnetic Fe3O4 NPs. Then, oleic acid was added and kept the solution for stirring under heating for 5 min followed by addition of C-dost with continued stirring under heating at 30 °C for another 15 min. Various ratios of oleic acid (50, 100, 200 and 300 μL) and carbon dots (200, 500, 1000, 2000 μL) were used to develop microbots. We found that 200 μL of oleic acid and 1 mL of C-Dots are suitable for stable and catalytic bots’ development. Later, the precipitated NPs were collected and dried in a hot air oven for 24 h. Further, these tiny dried particles (microbots) of almost spherical size and number were used for the bubble propulsion movement and dye removal.
UV–Visible spectroscopic recording was performed in PC based double beam spectrophotometer 2202 (Systronic, India), while fluorescence spectroscopy was performed in Analytical (FS 2060, India).

The Fe3O4 NPs and C-Dots were analysed by a high-resolution (TEM) (JEM 2100; Jeol, Peabody, MA, USA) operated at a maximum accelerating voltage of 200 keV. About 8 μL of solution was drop-casted onto a carbon coated copper TEM grid, then dehydrated and analysed using TEM.
The samples were mixed with KBr for making a pallet that was then analysed by PerkinElmer (Spectrum Two) Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer in the range 4000–400 cm−1.

The magnetic property of the microbots was measured at 300 K using a Vibrating sample Magnetometer. VSM was performed using a magnetometer (Quantum Design, USA, Model-VersaLab 3Tesla) with maximum current of 30 A, reading number 150, scan time 900 s at room temperature, and corresponding data were generated.

As a model system, MG dye was used to calculate kinetics and activation energy of the degradation using microbots. For that, 100 ppm of MG dye was incubated with 12 ± 3 microbots having fixed total weight of 15 mg and average size of each microbots were 950 μm ± 100 μm and 1% H2O2 at room temperature. Later, absorbance was taken at a regular interval of 30 min for 4 h. Kinetics was calculated with equation (7). For the calculation of activation energy, a similar setup was taken under different temperatures i.e. 298, 308 and 318 K for microbots (in 1% H2O2) and 1% H2O2 only. Later, absorbance was taken at a regular interval of 30 min for 4 h for each setup. Activation energy was graphically calculated with equation (8).

The C-Dots were prepared from potato peels– a common household organic waste that is very rich with carbohydrate molecules (e.g., starch) and other metabolites such as amino acids, vitamin and phenolic compounds (Sinha et al., 2020). The potato peel extracts (PPE) show natural antioxidant effects as it reduces at lipid peroxidation. Baked potatoes with skin contain vitamin B-6, vitamin C and thiamine (Navarre et al., 2016). Besides, potatoes contain high amounts of catalase enzymes that decompose H2O2 into oxygen and water (Hsieh et al., 2016). Hence, it will be very promising if this biocompatible, readily available and economical organic waste would turn into a useful product like C-Dots. Controlled caramelization of dried peels in the alkaline condition under pulsed microwave irradiation (800 W) for 2 min resulted in a black colour product. This was re-dispersed in distilled water followed by dialysis for 24 h to collect the purified fluorescent C-Dots. The UV–Vis spectrum showed a peak at 334 nm which are possibly due to n-π* transition (Figs. S1 and SI) (Verma et al., 2020). The fluorescence spectrum had a blue emission peak at 440 nm when excitation with 365 nm UV-light (Figs. S1 and SI). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images revealed spherical C-Dots of an average size of 4.47 ± 0.87 nm (Fig. 2 a; S2, SI). The FTIR spectrum of C-Dots revealed peaks at 3340 (-O-H), 2950 (-N-H), 2760 and 1330 cm−1 for C–H stretching (Figs. S4 and SI) (Atchudan et al., 2017). Further, to understand the surface chemistry of the C-Dots, an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed. Full high-resolution spectrum of XPS showed three elemental peaks that correspond to C1s, O1s, and N1s (Figs. S5 and SI). The presence of N1s peaks confirms the presence of traces of nitrogen in the C-Dots. The deconvoluted spectrum showed C1s peaks at 284.60, 286.30, and 287.80 eV which were due to carbon in the states of C–C/CC, C–N/CN/C–O, and CO/OC–N respectively (Fig. 2c) (Arshad and Sk, 2019). Likewise, the N1s spectrum showed peaks at around 399.60 and 398.20 eV these correspond to pyridinic nitrogen, C–N–C bond, respectively, and O1s showed peaks at 530.70 and 532.40 eV which may be assigned to CO and C–OH/C–O–C, respectively (Fig. 2d; 2e). The atomic percentage of C, O and N in C-Dots is 74.03%, 23.56%, 2.41%, respectively. The quantum yield of the C-Dots was 5.53% by considering quinine sulfate as standard (Tables S1 and SI). Thus, the present synthesis method is a rapid, one step technique of the formation of C-Dots by using common household waste as a precursor, which is thus suitable for large scale production. Likewise, earlier reports also demonstrated that caramelization of the food materials could be effective for synthesis of the C-dots and as-synthesized C-dots were found to be biocompatible (Sk et al., 2012).

The above synthesized C-Dots were used for the development of the microbots along with iron oxide nanoparticles (Fe3O4 NPs). However, as the C-Dots were highly water-soluble (i.e., polar), therefore it was not found to be suitable for the development of the microbots (i.e., propulsion in the aqueous medium) that ought to be insoluble in aqueous medium. Thus, C-Dots was added with precursors of iron oxide nanoparticles i.e., source of Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions along with oleic acid (i.e., non-polar) that provided required stability to the bots during the co-precipitation method of synthesis. Various ratios of the C-Dots and precursors of iron oxide nanoparticles were tried for the development of stable as well as catalytically efficient microbots. It would be worth mentioning that higher number of C-Dots destabilized the structure of the bots in aqueous medium possibly due to increase in the polarity in the composition, whereas higher concentrations of non-polar component i.e., oleic acid increased the stability but compromised the catalytic efficiency of the bots. It was found that nanocomposite comprised with 2:1 (w/w) ratio of C-Dots and precursors of iron oxide nanoparticles showed a reasonably good catalytic activity as well as magnetic property in an aqueous environment. The above nanocomposite (i.e., C-Dots and Fe3O4 NPs) used for the development of bots and characterized by using various analytical techniques. TEM images of the constituents of the bots showed the presence of both Fe3O4 NPs and C-Dots of an average size of 26.77 ± 7.68 nm (Fig. 2b; S2, SI). The Fe3O4 NPs are having a bigger average diameter as compared to C-Dots. The presence of Fe3O4 NPs was confirmed by powder X-Ray diffraction (PXRD) which showed peaks at 2θ values of 23.5°, 30.12°, 35.67°, 43.36°, 53.84°, 57.29°, 62.64°, 71.42° and 74.52° which corresponds to lattice planes of (111), (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), (440), (620) and (533) of Fe3O4, respectively (Figs. S3 and SI). The obtained XRD spectra were fairly close with the joint committee on powder diffraction standards (JCPDS) (File no.19-629) which insured the preparation of Fe3O4 with a high degree of purity (Gautam et al., 2020a). To endorse the magnetic property of the bots, Vibrating Sample Magnetometer (VSM) study was performed over the magnetic field −30 K Oe to +30 K Oe at room temperature which showed hysteresis behaviour of the microbots. Saturation value was found to be 26.7, representing a superparamagnetic nature of synthesized microbots (Figs. S6 and SI) (Namange et al., 2013).

Microbots made up with C-Dots and iron oxide NPs while placed in aqueous H2O2 solution, rapid oxygen bubble formation was observed. The rapid generation of these oxygen bubbles was due to the catalysis of H2O2 by C-Dots (Shi et al., 2011). This catalysis resulted in the formation of oxygen molecules and hydroxyl ions. However, Fe3O4 also helped in splitting of H2O2 into oxygen molecules and hydroxyl ions but at a much slower rate compared with C-Dots functionalized Fe3O4 NPs (Figs. S9 and SI) (Wang et al., 2012). Therefore, synergistic effect of C-Dots and Fe3O4 NPs enhance the catalytic activity of microbots.
(1)
H 2
O 2
→ nanobots
O
H −
+
O
H ∗
+
O 2
↑

After, about 5 min, the bubble was grown in size on the surface of microbots which eventually burst that generated thrust responsible for the propulsion of the bots. The direction of motion is decided by the recoil momentum of multiple bubbles bursting during the process and the magnitude of hydrodynamic forces. In different experimental setups, microbots were found to have motion in both lateral (Fig. 3 a; Video SV1, SI) as well as vertical movement (Fig. 3d; Video SV2a, SV2b, SI). The video of the lateral movement was recorded under Zoom Stereoscopic/Video microscope (Vaishekhi EIT-70004-E−11) with standard magnification of 50× and 1 mm grid. The trajectories and change in speed at different fuel concentration for lateral and vertical movements of the bots are shown in Fig. 3. Microbots showed catalytic activity even at H2O2 concentration as low as 0.5%. Interestingly, microbots were showing chemical stability, as fluorescence of the microbots remained unaltered after catalysis. Further, PXRD spectrum of used microbots was also recorded that did not show any noticeable changes in the position of the peaks, which implied the structural integrity of the prepared bots in the aqueous H2O2 medium (Figs. S10 and SI). The movement of the microbots could be due to resultant force of the various forces acting on it such as buoyancy, mass (i.e., gravitational force), viscous force and hydrodynamic force. Assuming the autonomously moving microbots in the aqueous solution to be an accelerated sphere, the volume and density of the bubble detaching from the surface of the microbot are considered as V
o and ρ
o respectively. The initial and final velocity of the bubble just after the detachment of the bubble are considered as u
i and u
f respectively. The initial velocity of the bubble just after the detachment was assumed as zero. The force responsible for setting up the motion of the microbot after time interval Δt of recoil thrust from the bubble detachment is given as (Dey et al., 2011):
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jenvman.2021.113322

The following is/are the supplementary data related to this article:

The following is/are the supplementary data related to this article:
Multimedia component 4
(2)
f =
ρ o
V o
(
u f
−
u i
)
Δ t

If mass and velocity of the microbot was m and v respectively, the balancing force acting on the autonomously moving microbot after time interval Δt
n is given as (Dey et al., 2011):
(3)
f −
F v
= m
(
v − 0
Δ t
n
)

Here, F
v is an effective drag force acting on the microbot. The quantitative estimation of effective drag force acting on the microbot is the sum of three different forces. These forces are steady state viscous drag force acting opposite to the direction of the microbot (F
1 ), mass component that has the magnitude equivalent to the resistance offered by an accelerated sphere in irrotational motion (F
2 ), and basset force to estimate the effect of history of acceleration (F
3 ) (Dey et al., 2011). The resultant force may be calculated by considering Stokes Drag Theory (Dey et al., 2011). Here force acting on a microbot moving in 2% H2O2 solution was calculated using (refer to SI):
(4)
F v
=
F 1
+
F 2
+
F 3
(5)
F v
= 6 π
η Rv
+
2 3
π
R 3
ρ a + 6
R 2
π η ρ
a
∫ 0 t
d
t ′
t −
t ′

The theoretical study to understand the net forces acting on the movement of the microbot was calculated using abovementioned parameter under certain assumptions to create a simpler situation for calculation, without losing the essence of the problem to be solved (refer to SI). Therefore, the shape of the microbot was considered as a sphere and the net drag force acting in an accelerated sphere was found to be 2.38 × 10−5 N.

While the height of the fluid column was less, a lateral movement was observed, this was due to the disruption of the bubbles mainly developed around the side surface of the bots. Although, it was observed that along with the height of the fluid column, many other factors such as size, weight and catalysis of microbots influence the type of the movements. For example, while we have taken microbot of having a mass of 940 ± 313 μg of an average size of 900 ± 150 μm, the average horizontal speed of the microbots was found to be 86.11 ± 10 μm/s in 2% of H2O2 solution. It is worth mentioning that the speed of the bots increases due to an increase in the concentration of H2O2. The movement of the bots is a conversion of the chemical energy to mechanical energy. The energy efficiency was 59.11% which was calculated on the basis of consumption of the H2O2 and distance travelled by the bots (refer to SI). The exact concentration of the H2O2 was measured by using titration with KMnO4 using standard titration method. The energy efficiency for the present system was found to be better than previous studies (Shivalkar et al., 2021). On the other hand, while the height of the fluid column is sufficient, a vertical movement was observed. This is due to the bubble growing mainly at the bottom of microbots while it increases to a critical size where buoyancy (up-thrust) becomes more dominant than overall weight of microbots and attached bubbles. These air bubbles took the bots up and burst as soon as they got in contact with the liquid-air interface. This is because of higher surface tension at the liquid surface compared to air bubbles as described by previous studies (Tomiyama et al., 2002). After disruption of bubbles, the microbots immediately fall back under gravity and the process is repeated until the catalytic strength of microbots is exhausted. For the in-vitro study, we have performed degradation of the dye within a cuvette (3 mL) where the vertical movement was predominant. During this experiment, microbots of a mass of 12.0 ± 2.0 mg (i.e., 8–10 microbots) of average size 950 ± 50 μm were taken. In this case, the average speed of the microbots was found to be 110 ± 10 μm/s in 1% of H2O2 solution. The average frequency (one upwards and downwards movement was considered as a complete cycle of movement) of the bots was 26 ± 6 s−1. The frequency of the bots increased due to an increase in the concentration of the H2O2. It was observed that different numbers of microbots of the approximately same size and mass were showing different speed and possibly due to variation in catalytic activity on interfacial tension, number of catalytic sites, and bubble formation and detachment rate (Wang et al., 2016; Gautam et al., 2020b). Moreover, smaller microbots move faster than comparatively larger one which is corroborated with previous studies (Wang et al., 2016). This is possibly due to the larger surfaces to volume ratio which facilitates better catalysis.

These microbots had potential of catalysis while autonomously moving in the aqueous solution of H2O2 were employed for degradation of toxic dyes like malachite green (MG), Sudan-I (S–I) and Rhodamine-B (Rh–B) (Fig. 4 ; Video SV3, SI). Out of these three dyes, MG and Rh–B are hydrophilic dye while S–I is a sparingly soluble dye. To study the dye degradation, 4 mL of an aqueous solution containing 10 ppm of MG dye (λabs = 617 nm), S–I dye (λabs = 476 nm) and Rh–B (λabs = 554 nm) with 1% H2O2 was incubated with average 10 microbots (Figs. S7 and SI) (Zhang et al., 2015; Radoń et al., 2019). After about 5–7 min, microbots showed upwards movement. The concentration of dyes present in the solution was estimated by probing the absorbance of each of the dyes by using UV–Vis spectrophotometer at a regular time interval for 4 h. The higher concentration of 100 ppm MG dye was also found proportionally degrading with the same amount of microbots (Fig. S8). It was observed that the degradation rate differs significantly for all the dyes under investigation. In abovementioned similar conditions, more than 80% of MG dye was degraded in around 2 h and more than 70% Rh–B dye degraded in around 4 h while S–I dye showed the slowest degradation rate (Fig. 4a; 4 b). It was predicated that degradation of MG was initiated with the attack of ●OH radical and formed (4-(dimethylamino) cyclohexa-2,5-dien-1-yl) (4-(dimethylamino) phenyl) (phenyl) methanol which further degraded to simpler intermediates (Saad et al., 2020). In case of Rh–B, the reactive species ●OH induced the degradation of Rh–B via various steps like N-de-ethylation, chromophore cleavage, ring opening and finally mineralized into simpler CO2, H2O, NH4+ etc (Zhou et al., 2020). Similarly, degradation of S–I was due to oxidation, initiated with hydroxylation reaction by ●OH radical. Herein, the ●OH radical attacked on azo group of S–I and two major oxidative metabolites namely 6-OH S–I and 4-OH S–I were formed. The formed metabolites were further degraded to form hydroxylamine (Gautam et al., 2020b).

As a model system, MG dye was used to determine the kinetics of dye degradation by microbots. The control degradation experiment of abovementioned dyes was carried out in the absence of microbots. Previous reports demonstrated that H2O2 splits into hydroxyl ions which eventually induce the oxidation of these dyes to carbon dioxide and water molecules (Habib et al., 2020). A catalytic experiment performed in the presence of microbots, degradation of the dye was found to be very fast. This is possibly due to the high catalytic activity of the C-Dots smeared on the surface of microbots as well as the movement of the bots which caused continuous agitation of the solution without any external mechanical force. The decreasing concentration of dyes as a function of time was used to find out the rate of MG dye degradation. The ascertained experimental data was fitted to first-order kinetic model, represented as:
(6)
−
d
C t
dt
= k
(
C t
)

The linear representation of the above Equation is:
(7)
ln
(
C t
)
= kt + ln
(
C 0
)
where k represents the first-order degradation rate constant (min−1) and it has been evaluated from the slope by plotting ln (C
t ) vs time (t), where C
t is the concentration of dye in the solution (mg/L) at time t and C
0 is the initial concentration of dye (t = 0). The rate constant of MG dye degradation at room temperature was found to be 1.26 × 10−2min−1 ±0.16 × 10−2min−1 (refer to SI) (Figs. S14 and SI). The high correlation coefficient value (R2 = 0.97) verified the suitability of the first-order kinetics for the present degradation system. Furthermore, temperature dependent rate constant values were utilized to compute activation energy by using Arrhenius model (Tables S2 and SI):
(8)
lnk = lnA −
E a
/ RT
Where T is the absolute temperature in kelvin, R is the gas constant and A is the pre-exponential factor. Slope of the linear plot of ln(k) versus 1/T was used as ‒(slope × R) in order to compute the values of E
a . Lower the values of E
a , the higher is the rate of degradation. A comparison between H2O2 individually and microbots facilitated MG dye degradation was carried out (refer to SI). It was found that activation energy was significantly reduced when microbots were employed for dye degradation (Fig. 4c). This indicated that the addition of microbots significantly enhanced the degradation of the dye.

Industrial effluents or wastewater released into the water bodies contain mixture of dyes. Therefore, it is important to examine the performance of microbots for the mixture of more than one dye, necessary for its practical applicability. In response to this concern, the catalytic efficiency of designed microbots was also tested for the binary solution of MG and Rh–B. Experiment showed that nearly 66.09% and 57.54% degradation was achieved for MG and Rh–B, respectively in 4 h with 10 ppm of the initial concentration of dye mixture (Fig. 4d). The decrease in the rate of degradation can be explained in terms of competitive degradation between MG and Rh–B. Both the dyes release cations upon dissolution in water and compete for the binding at catalytic sites of the microbot (Ma and Dougherty, 1997; Kaushik et al., 2021). However, we achieved satisfactory and higher degradation which supports the possible application of the present microbots for real wastewater. It was also observed that the degradation rate of all the dyes was high with the rising temperature. Higher temperature causes a reduction in viscosity of the solvent (Khan et al., 2019). This expedites the rate of mass transfer of solute molecules towards the solid-liquid interface. It also allows the faster movement of microbots in the wastewater and facilitates the greater interaction between pollutant molecules and bots. This is another ascription for the higher rate of degradation of dyes at elevated temperature. Furthermore, the degradation of the dyes led to formation of colour-less/partially colourless solution (decrease in absorbance) which was further confirmed by mass spectra (Figs. S11 and SI; S12, SI; S13, SI) Sharma et al. (2019); Radoń et al. (2019); Khandekar et al. (2019)].

To find out the efficacy of the microbots in dye degradation even under different viscous environments, experiments were conducted in presence of various concentrations of glycerol. Microbots were allowed to move in presence of 5% (v/v) and 10% (v/v) glycerol (viscosity of ~0.07 Pa and ~0.15 Pa respectively as described by previous studies) while keeping the concentration of dye (10 ppm) and H2O2 (1%) unchanged (Ferreira et al., 2017). It was observed that the frequency of the bots was decreased as the concentration of glycerol was increased. Consequently, the rate of dye degradation also became slower as compared to without glycerol which was confirmed by measuring the absorbance of the MG dye after 2 h (Fig. 5 a; Fig. S15; Video SV4). Reusability of catalysts is a considerable issue as far as the economic viability of the degradation process is concerned. Therefore, recyclability experiments of the spent microbots were also conducted. Since the microbots were made up with magnetic NPs with the anticipation that these bots would be re-use after completing the first round of degradation of dye. By applying the bar magnet, the microbots were collected and used for the next round of dye degradation. It has been observed that the rate of catalysis of microbots decreased around 35 ± 5% which was calculated based on catalysis data (absorbance of dye) after 2 h of each of the reactions. The experiment revealed that microbots could be successfully recycled multiple times without any significant loss in their catalytic efficiency (Fig. 5b). Recovery and reusability of microbots are energy-efficient concepts with a large throughput.
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jenvman.2021.113322

The following is/are the supplementary data related to this article:
Multimedia component 5 Multimedia component 5
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jenvman.2021.113322

The following is/are the supplementary data related to this article:
Multimedia component 6 Multimedia component 6

In brief, we reported the facile production of economical and reusable Fe3O4 magnetic microbots coated with C-Dots were developed for degradation of the toxic hydrophilic and sparingly water-soluble toxic dyes. A Biological waste material, discarded potato peel was used to synthesize C-dots. The designed microbots were able to swim autonomously a large distance in viscous medium by using H2O2 as fuel. The role of microbots as a nanozyme was proven by calculating activation energies from the separate experiments performed in the absence and presence of microbots. The speed of fabricated microbots was found to be satisfactory over different viscosity range with a minimum fuel requirement. Almost complete degradation of malachite green was achieved within 360 min of reaction time. Temperature dependent catalytic experiment demonstrated that high temperature favoured the extent of degradation. The exclusive features of the developed microbots like low-cost preparation, recyclability, easy recovery from wastewater by an external magnet and controlled navigation make them a promising material for water remediation. Thus, present bots may be a viable alternative of metal nanoparticle based nanobots that releases toxic metal ions and could be harnessed for the treatment of textile effluent as well as coloured effluent released from various industries for large scale water remediation. Moreover, the overall protocol for fabrication of microbots is free from hazardous and toxic chemicals and does not impose any secondary pollution to the water bodies.

Saurabh Shivalkar conducted all the experiments, mathematical calculations and prepared the manuscript, Pavan Kumar Gautam assisted in the calculation of kinetics and activation energy of dye degradation, Arushi Verma assisted in the preparation of C-Dots, Krishna Maurya assisted in the dye degradation experiment and calculation. Md Palashuddin Sk has done the formal analysis and validation of the data in the manuscript. Sintu Kumar Samanta has done the formal analysis of the manuscript. Amaresh Kumar Sahoo has done the conceptualization, reviewing and editing of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are thankful for the financial support from DBT, Govt. of India (SANCTION No. BT/PR40544/COD/139/14/2020). Author, also acknowledges the Department of Chemistry [DRS-II(SAP), DST (FIST & PURSE) funded] and University Sophisticated Instrument Facility (USIF), Aligarh Muslim University. Dr. Pavan Kumar Gautam greatful to Science and Engineering Research Board, Govt. of India for the NPDF (Grant No. PDF/2016/002910).

ce:section.ce:para:
